items.q: What is the difference between weather and climate?
items.correct: Weather is short-term atmospheric conditions at a specific time and place; climate is the long-term (typically 30-year) average of weather patterns in a region.
items.misconception: Weather happens outside and climate happens globally.
items.random_wrong: Weather refers to rain and climate refers to temperature.
items.why: Students treat them as different phenomena rather than different timescales. The key distinction is time — climate is statistical, weather is instantaneous.

items.q: Why do people move from rural to urban areas in LICs?
items.correct: A combination of push factors (rural poverty, lack of services, agricultural change) and pull factors (perceived jobs, education, healthcare) — though reality often disappoints.
items.misconception: Because cities in poor countries are always better than the countryside.
items.random_wrong: Because the government forces them to move to cities.
items.why: Migrants often end up in shanty towns worse than what they left. The decision is based on perception of opportunity, not guaranteed improvement — a key nuance students miss.

items.q: What is the difference between a four-figure and six-figure grid reference?
items.correct: Four-figure references identify a 1km square (eastings then northings); six-figure references pinpoint a location within that square to 100m accuracy by adding tenths.
items.misconception: Six-figure references are just longer versions of the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Four-figure references are for OS maps and six-figure references are for atlases.
items.why: Accuracy differs by an order of magnitude. Students forget the 'along the corridor and up the stairs' order (eastings before northings) — a common exam error costing easy marks.
It's 67 million. “47 million” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which UK nation has the largest population?
items.why: Correct answer: England. “Scotland” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Approximate percentage of UK population living in urban areas?
items.why: Exact answer: 84. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
